input.initialPrompt:
I'm setting up .NET MAUI development on Windows 11 from scratch. I need
to target both Android and Windows. I can't run commands right now — give
me a complete step-by-step plan with all the PowerShell commands I'll need,
including how to discover the right versions dynamically.

data.content:
Here's a complete plan for setting up .NET MAUI on Windows 11 (Android + Windows targets).

---

## Step 1 — Install .NET SDK

Download and install the latest .NET SDK (MAUI requires .NET 8+):

```powershell
# Check if dotnet is already installed
dotnet --list-sdks

# Install via winget (preferred)
winget install Microsoft.DotNet.SDK.9
```

## Step 2 — Install MAUI Workload

```powershell
# List available workloads to find the exact MAUI workload name
dotnet workload search maui

# Install the MAUI workload (includes Android, Windows, iOS stubs)
dotnet workload install maui

# Verify it installed
dotnet workload list
```

## Step 3 — Install Visual Studio Build Tools (Windows target)

Windows targeting requires the Windows App SDK and WinUI, which come via VS components:

```powershell
# Install VS 2022 Build Tools with required components
winget install Microsoft.VisualStudio.2022.Community

# Or if you already have VS, modify it via command line:
# Launch the installer to add these workload IDs:
#   - microsoft.visualstudio.workload.maui
#   - microsoft.visualstudio.workload.nativedesktop (for WinUI/WindowsAppSDK)

& "C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft Visual Studio\Installer\vs_installer.exe" modify `
  --installPath "C:\Program Files\Microsoft Visual Studio\2022\Community" `
  --add Microsoft.VisualStudio.Workload.NetCrossPlat `
  --add Microsoft.VisualStudio.ComponentGroup.Maui.All `
  --quiet --norestart
```

## Step 4 — Set Up Android SDK & JDK

The MAUI workload installs Android SDK pieces, but you can manage them explicitly:

```powershell
# See what the MAUI workload already installed
dotnet workload list

# The Android SDK is typically placed here after workload install:
# $env:LOCALAPPDATA\Microsoft\Android\sdk
# Verify:
Test-Path "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Microsoft\Android\sdk"

# Discover installed Android platform versions
& "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Microsoft\Android\sdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" --list

# Install a specific Android platform (pick from --list output)
& "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Microsoft\Android\sdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" `
  "platforms;android-35" "build-tools;35.0.0"

# Accept all licenses
& "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Microsoft\Android\sdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" --licenses
```

**JDK** — MAUI bundles Microsoft OpenJDK. Verify:

```powershell
# Discover the bundled JDK location
Get-ChildItem "$env:ProgramFiles\Microsoft\jdk-*" -Directory | Sort-Object Name -Descending | Select-Object -First 1

# Set JAVA_HOME if needed
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("JAVA_HOME",
  (Get-ChildItem "$env:ProgramFiles\Microsoft\jdk-*" -Directory |
   Sort-Object Name -Descending | Select-Object -First 1).FullName,
  "User")
```

## Step 5 — Set Up Android Emulator

```powershell
$sdkMgr = "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Microsoft\Android\sdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat"

# List available system images
& $sdkMgr --list 2>&1 | Select-String "system-images"

# Install an image (pick from above)
& $sdkMgr "system-images;android-35;google_apis;x86_64" "emulator"

# Create an AVD
$avdMgr = "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Microsoft\Android\sdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\avdmanager.bat"
& $avdMgr create avd -n "Pixel_MAUI" -k "system-images;android-35;google_apis;x86_64" --device "pixel_6"

# Verify
& $avdMgr list avd
```

> **Prerequisite:** Enable **Hyper-V** or **WHPX** for emulator acceleration:
> ```powershell
> # Run as Administrator
> Enable-WindowsOptionalFeature -Online -FeatureName Microsoft-Hyper-V-All -NoRestart
> ```

## Step 6 — Enable Developer Mode (Windows target)

```powershell
# Required for sideloading WinUI apps during development
reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\AppModelUnlock" `
  /v AllowDevelopmentWithoutDevLicense /t REG_DWORD /d 1 /f
```

## Step 7 — Create and Test a Project

```powershell
# Scaffold a new MAUI app
dotnet new maui -n MyMauiApp
cd MyMauiApp

# Build for Windows
dotnet build -f net9.0-windows10.0.19041.0

# Build for Android
dotnet build -f net9.0-android

# Run on Windows
dotnet run -f net9.0-windows10.0.19041.0

# Run on Android emulator (start emulator first)
& "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Microsoft\Android\sdk\emulator\emulator.exe" -avd Pixel_MAUI &
dotnet run -f net9.0-android
```

## Step 8 — Diagnostic Check

```powershell
# All-in-one health check (catches missing components)
dotnet workload install maui-check 2>$null
maui-check

# Or use the built-in doctor (newer SDKs)
dotnet workload repair
```

---

**Version discovery cheatsheet:**

| What | Command |
|---|---|
| .NET SDKs | `dotnet --list-sdks` |
| Workloads | `dotnet workload list` |
| Android platforms | `sdkmanager.bat --list` |
| Target frameworks | `dotnet new maui --list` then check `.csproj` |
| JDK version | `java -version` |

Run the steps in order. Steps 1–3 are the heavy installs; steps 4–6 are config; 7–8 verify everything works.
data.reasoningText: The user wants a complete step-by-step plan for setting up .NET MAUI development on Windows 11, targeting Android and Windows. They can't run commands right now, so they want all the PowerShell commands laid out. Let me provide a comprehensive guide.
